Package io.mdudel.zenoh.purejava.scouting

Class PureJavaZenohScout

java.lang.Object
io.mdudel.zenoh.purejava.scouting.PureJavaZenohScout
All Implemented Interfaces:
AutoCloseable
public final class PureJavaZenohScout extends Object implements AutoCloseable
Pure-Java Zenoh 1.x scouting client.

Listens for HELLO messages on the Zenoh multicast group (default udp/224.0.0.224:7446) and optionally emits SCOUT messages periodically to prod silent nodes into replying. Builds a live registry of DiscoveredNodes that developers can consume either reactively (via ScoutListener) or with a pull-model snapshot().

Not a peer, not a router, not a session

This class is a passive observer. It never opens a TCP/TLS/WS session, never sends INIT, never declares itself a Zenoh node. Routers and peers on the mesh see it as (at most) a source of SCOUT queries; if PureJavaZenohScout.Mode.PASSIVE is selected, they don't see it at all.

Modes

  • PureJavaZenohScout.Mode.PASSIVE — listen only, never emit. Some routers advertise themselves via periodic multicast HELLO on their own, so passive discovery still works, just slower and only for actively-broadcasting nodes.
  • PureJavaZenohScout.Mode.ACTIVE — listen AND periodically emit SCOUT messages every scoutInterval to prompt any node whose role bit is set in the matcher to reply with a HELLO. This is the default and matches the zenoh scout CLI behaviour. Active always includes listening; you cannot receive replies to your SCOUTs without a socket bound to the multicast group.

Multi-NIC

If networkInterfaces is not set, the scout binds one socket per non-loopback, up, multicast-capable interface it can find. Loopback is skipped by default because most operating systems do not deliver multicast to 127.0.0.1 even when both sender and receiver are on the same host; see the sample README for the workaround.

Threading

  • One reader daemon thread per NIC socket.
  • One scheduler thread for the periodic SCOUT emission and the staleness sweeper.
  • ScoutListener callbacks fire from the reader thread that received the packet, under the registry lock. They are totally ordered per ZID.
  • snapshot() is safe from any thread and takes a shallow copy of the registry.
